---
title: "Emily: Slide Creation, Teaching, and Research Workflows"
subtitle: "AI Bootcamp — Session 2"
author: Emily Beam
date: 2026-04-27
format:
  revealjs: default
---

# Overview

## Four concrete use cases

Today is built around workflows that many faculty already do:

- slide creation
- teaching applications
- conducting data analysis
- a few additional repeatable use cases

The point is not one magic prompt. The point is a documented workflow from starting materials to a usable output.

# Slides

## From rough notes to usable slides

This is a good AI task because slide drafting is repetitive but still judgment-heavy.

- start from notes, a memo, or a rough outline
- ask for a slide sequence before asking for slide text
- turn long prose into headlines plus a few bullets
- tighten wording after you see the structure

## A good slide workflow

For bigger presentation tasks, a useful pattern is:

`prompt` -> `plan` -> `review` -> `clear` -> `draft` -> `review again`

That helps you avoid one long, messy conversation.

## What still needs you

- deciding the audience and tone
- choosing what to cut
- deciding which examples actually land
- replacing generic bullets with your real content

# Teaching

## Best low-risk teaching uses

The most useful teaching tasks are usually drafting and revision tasks:

- assignment prompt first draft
- rubric scaffolding
- study guides
- practice questions
- editing for clarity

## Example: literature review to teaching memo

One teaching-side example is a literature review workflow:

- start from a topic, question, or pile of readings
- organize themes and compare papers
- draft a synthesis, annotated bibliography, or discussion memo
- turn scattered notes into something teachable

## Example: assignment and rubric workflow

Another strong teaching workflow is:

- draft an assignment prompt from learning goals
- revise for clarity and course level
- build a rubric with criteria and performance levels
- use the rubric to draft feedback comments

## What not to outsource

- final grading judgment
- course alignment and standards
- fairness decisions
- anything involving sensitive student situations

# Research

## Raw data to report

This is the research-side workflow:

- start with uncleaned data
- make a plan
- write code
- build on existing code
- execute
- review results
- write a short report

## Workflow in one line

For this kind of task, the workflow is:

`plan` -> `write code` -> `build on existing code` -> `run` -> `review` -> `report`

Each step is narrow enough to review, rerun, and improve.

## What AI is good at here

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Accelerates**

- data orientation
- candidate analyses
- boilerplate code
- building on existing code
- first-draft reporting
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Still needs you**

- choosing the question
- deciding whether the plan is sensible
- checking code
- interpreting the results
- knowing when something is wrong
:::
::::

## Why screenshots are the right format here

For the workshop, this demo is mostly pre-done.

- show prompts and saved outputs
- walk through the logic of each step
- avoid spending lunch watching code run
- keep attention on the workflow rather than the wait time

## Research guardrails

- verify that the prompt matches the actual task
- inspect code before you trust it
- do not confuse polished prose with correctness
- if the data are sensitive, redesign the workflow instead of forcing it

A safer pattern for sensitive work is often:

- let the tool write code
- review the code yourself
- review logs and outputs rather than exposing raw protected data

# Extras

## Writing and admin

Useful bounded tasks include:

- rewrite an email to be shorter, warmer, and clearer
- summarize meeting notes into decisions, action items, and open questions
- extract deadlines from a document into a table
- turn rough notes into a clean markdown memo

## Project management and workflow control

AI is also useful for keeping work organized:

- make a step-by-step plan before starting
- keep a running list of decisions, open questions, and next steps
- summarize project state so you can reopen it later
- create a reusable prompt for a recurring task

## Compare outputs and use disagreement

One useful advanced pattern is not just "do the task," but:

- ask for two approaches
- compare outputs
- send one version to stress-test the other
- use disagreement as a signal to review more carefully

## What to try next

- try one slide-creation task from your own rough notes
- try one bounded teaching task such as a rubric or study guide
- try one small raw-data task with saved outputs and screenshots
- save prompts and outputs so you can reuse what works
